title: The Changing Economic World: Development
examTip: Development is not just about money — always combine economic AND social indicators in your answers. The HDI is better than GNI per capita alone because it measures three dimensions of development.
introTips: Know at least two economic indicators (GNI per capita, GDP) and two social indicators (life expectancy, infant mortality rate).
Remember that HDI combines income, life expectancy and education — it's a composite measure.
For 'why does the development gap exist?' — always give physical AND human causes.
intro: Why do people in some countries live into their 80s while in others the average is 55? Why does a child born in Niger have a 1 in 10 chance of dying before their first birthday, while in Japan it's 1 in 500? Development geography tries to answer these questions — and understanding the causes of inequality and how we try to close the gap is at the heart of AQA Paper 2.

keyPoints.heading: What is development and how do we measure it?
keyPoints.content: Development means improvement in people's quality of life — both economic and social. Economic indicators measure wealth: GNI per capita (Gross National Income per person — the total income of a country divided by its population) is the most commonly used. The USA has a GNI per capita of ~$70,000; the UK ~$47,000; Nigeria ~$2,100; Niger ~$590. Social indicators measure wellbeing: life expectancy (Japan: 84 years; Sierra Leone: 55 years); infant mortality rate (IMR — deaths per 1,000 live births: Chad 103, UK 4); literacy rate; access to clean water; doctors per 1,000 people. The HDI (Human Development Index) is a composite measure combining income + life expectancy + education — it's better than a single indicator but still ignores inequality, women's rights and environmental sustainability. The Happy Planet Index adds wellbeing and ecological footprint.
writeBullets: Development — improvement in quality of life; economic and social dimensions
GNI per capita — most used economic indicator; USA $70k, UK $47k, Nigeria $2.1k, Niger $590
Life expectancy — Japan 84, Sierra Leone 55; measures healthcare and living standards
Infant mortality rate (IMR) — deaths per 1,000 live births; Chad 103, UK 4
HDI — UN composite: income + life expectancy + education; better than single indicators
Happy Planet Index — adds wellbeing and environmental sustainability to HDI
keyPoints.memTip: GNI counts the cash; HDI adds health and learning. One number vs three dimensions.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Explain WHY composite measures are better — they capture more of what development means. Single indicators can be misleading.

keyPoints.heading: Development categories and the Brandt Line
keyPoints.content: The World Bank classifies countries by income: HICs (High Income Countries — e.g., UK, USA, Japan), UMICs (Upper Middle Income — e.g., Brazil, China), LMICs (Lower Middle Income — e.g., Nigeria, India) and LICs (Low Income Countries — e.g., Niger, Malawi). AQA also uses NEEs (Newly Emerging Economies) — countries with rapid economic growth, large populations and growing global influence, such as China, India, Brazil, South Africa, Nigeria and Mexico. The Brandt Line (proposed 1980) drew a broad north-south divide across the globe, with 'North' being HICs and 'South' being LICs. However, this is now outdated: many NEEs in the 'south' (e.g., China, Brazil) are now among the world's largest economies, and Australia and New Zealand (in the geographic south) were always in the 'rich north' category.
writeBullets: World Bank categories — HICs, UMICs, LMICs, LICs (based on GNI per capita)
NEEs — Newly Emerging Economies: China, India, Brazil, Nigeria, South Africa, Mexico
NEEs — rapid economic growth, large populations, growing global influence
Brandt Line (1980) — broad north-south divide; 'North' = rich, 'South' = poor
Brandt Line is now outdated — China and Brazil (in south) are major economic powers
keyPoints.memTip: HIC, UMIC, LMIC, LIC — from high to low income; NEEs are the fast movers in between.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget to critique the Brandt Line — stating it's outdated earns evaluation marks.

keyPoints.heading: Why does the development gap exist?
keyPoints.content: The development gap between rich and poor countries has multiple causes. Historical: colonialism — European powers extracted raw materials, established unfair trade relationships, drew arbitrary borders cutting across ethnic groups, and undermined local industries (e.g., British destroyed Indian cotton weaving to sell Lancashire textiles). Trade disadvantage: LICs export cheap primary products (coffee, copper, cocoa) and import expensive manufactured goods — and commodity prices are volatile and have declined relative to manufactured goods over time. Debt cycle: many LICs borrowed heavily from the IMF and World Bank in the 1970s–80s at high interest rates; debt repayments consume money that could fund schools and hospitals. Physical factors: landlocked countries face higher trade costs; poor soils and unreliable rainfall limit agriculture; natural disasters damage infrastructure. Political factors: corruption diverts public money; conflict destroys infrastructure and deters investment.
writeBullets: Colonialism — extraction of resources, unfair trade, arbitrary borders, undermined local industries
Trade disadvantage — LICs export cheap primary products, import expensive manufactured goods
Debt cycle — IMF/World Bank loans in 1970s–80s; high interest; repayments drain government budgets
Physical factors — landlocked, poor soils, unreliable rainfall, natural disaster vulnerability
Political factors — corruption diverts money; conflict destroys infrastructure and deters investment
Terms of trade — prices of primary commodities fall over time relative to manufactured goods
keyPoints.memTip: CTDPP — Colonialism, Trade, Debt, Physical, Political. Five causes, five letters.
keyPoints.tutor_note: For 9-mark answers, use this as your structure — one paragraph per cause group, with evidence.

keyPoints.heading: Strategies to reduce the development gap
keyPoints.content: There are multiple strategies used to try to close the development gap. Aid: bilateral (government to government), multilateral (through UN or World Bank), or voluntary (NGOs like Oxfam). Short-term emergency aid vs long-term development aid. Criticisms: dependency, inappropriate projects, corruption. Trade: the Fairtrade scheme guarantees farmers an above-market price plus a Fairtrade Premium for community projects (schools, clean water). Criticisms: does money reach farmers? Benefits limited to certified producers. Debt relief: the HIPC initiative (Heavily Indebted Poor Countries — IMF/World Bank) cancelled debts for 36 qualifying countries; Uganda used freed money to abolish school fees. Intermediate technology: Practical Action designs technology matching local skills and resources (e.g., solar-powered water pumps, improved cookstoves). Microfinance: Grameen Bank (Bangladesh) provides small loans to very poor people — mainly women — to start businesses; 97% repayment rate. Tourism: brings income and jobs but profits often 'leak' to TNCs.
writeBullets: Aid — bilateral, multilateral, voluntary/NGO; emergency vs development; criticised for dependency
Fairtrade — guaranteed above-market price + Premium for community; questions about reach
HIPC debt relief — 36 countries' debts cancelled; Uganda abolished school fees with freed money
Intermediate technology — Practical Action; appropriate tools matching local skills (e.g., solar pumps)
Microfinance — Grameen Bank Bangladesh; small loans mainly to women; 97% repayment
Tourism — brings income and jobs but profits may leak to multinational hotel chains
keyPoints.memTip: ATDIM-T — Aid, Trade (Fairtrade), Debt relief, Intermediate tech, Microfinance, Tourism.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Always evaluate each strategy — giving both advantages and limitations earns higher marks.

keyPoints.heading: Is the development gap closing?
keyPoints.content: The picture is mixed. For NEEs — especially China, India, and Brazil — the gap with HICs is narrowing dramatically. China alone has lifted an estimated 800 million people out of extreme poverty since 1980, the fastest reduction in history. India's economic growth has similarly transformed living standards for hundreds of millions. However, for many sub-Saharan African LICs, the gap may be widening. The SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals — UN, agreed 2015, 17 goals to achieve by 2030) aim to end poverty, hunger, inequality and environmental degradation globally. Progress has been made: the share of the world's population living in extreme poverty (under $2.15/day) fell from 36% in 1990 to 8.6% in 2022. However, COVID-19 reversed some gains, and progress is concentrated in Asia — sub-Saharan Africa lags behind on most SDG targets.
writeBullets: NEEs closing the gap — China lifted 800m out of poverty since 1980; India rapid growth
Sub-Saharan Africa LICs — gap may be widening despite global progress
Extreme poverty fell from 36% (1990) to 8.6% (2022) — global progress clear
SDGs — 17 UN goals agreed 2015, target 2030; cover poverty, education, health, environment
COVID-19 set back progress in many LICs — gains reversed in 2020–21
Progress uneven — concentrated in Asia; Africa lags on most SDG indicators
keyPoints.memTip: China soared, Africa stalled — the gap is closing for NEEs, widening for many LICs.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The SDGs give a framework for evaluation questions — use them to assess progress or lack of it.

tables.title: Development indicators comparison: LIC, NEE and HIC

0.question: Why is the HDI considered a better measure of development than GNI per capita alone?
options: Because it combines income, life expectancy and education into one composite measure, capturing more of what development means
Because it only uses economic data which is more reliable than social data
Because it was designed by the World Bank, which has access to better data than the UN
Because it measures development in a single country over time rather than comparing countries
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. HDI is a composite measure — it brings together GNI per capita, life expectancy and education. This means a country can't have a high HDI just by being wealthy; people also need long lives and access to education.
0.wrongFeedback: Not quite. HDI is better because it's a composite measure combining income, health (life expectancy) and education. GNI per capita only tells you about money — not whether people live long, healthy, educated lives.

2.question: Which of the following is a human cause of the development gap?
options: The debt cycle — LICs borrowed from the IMF at high interest in the 1970s and debt repayments drain government budgets
Landlocked location — being far from the coast increases trade costs and limits economic development
Poor soils — low soil fertility limits agricultural productivity and food security
Unreliable rainfall — drought and flood damage crops and limits farming income
2.correctFeedback: Correct! The debt cycle is a human (economic/political) cause. LICs borrowed heavily in the 1970s and the interest payments drain money that could fund schools and hospitals.
2.wrongFeedback: That's a physical cause. The debt cycle is a human cause — LICs borrowed from the IMF and World Bank in the 1970s at high interest, and debt repayments consume money that could fund development.

commonMistakes: Using GDP rather than GNI per capita — AQA mostly uses GNI per capita; GDP is total output within a country's borders, GNI includes income from abroad. Use the right term.
Saying HDI is 'better than GNI' without explaining why — always say it's a composite of THREE dimensions: income, health (life expectancy) and education.
Describing the Brandt Line as still accurate — always note that it's outdated; NEEs like China and Brazil (in the geographic south) are now major economic powers.
Forgetting to evaluate strategies — all development strategies have criticisms (aid creates dependency, Fairtrade benefits are limited, tourism profits leak). Always include evaluation.
Confusing infant mortality rate and life expectancy — IMR measures deaths in the first year of life (per 1,000 births); life expectancy is the average age people live to.
examTips: A 9-mark development gap answer needs: historical causes + physical causes + human causes, each with evidence, then an evaluative conclusion on which is most significant.
For questions on strategies, use the ATDIM-T structure (Aid, Trade/Fairtrade, Debt relief, Intermediate tech, Microfinance, Tourism) and evaluate each briefly.

When quoting development indicator data, always state the country and the figure together — 'Niger has a GNI per capita of ~$590, compared to $47,000 in the UK' is much stronger than 'some countries are poor'.
The SDGs (2015, 17 goals, target 2030) are a good framework for answering questions about international cooperation and whether progress is being made.
revisionCardBullets: Development = improvement in quality of life; economic + social dimensions
GNI per capita — most used economic indicator; UK $47k, Niger $590
HDI — composite measure: income + life expectancy + education (UN measure)
World Bank categories: HIC, UMIC, LMIC, LIC
NEEs — Newly Emerging Economies: China, India, Brazil, Nigeria; rapid growth
Brandt Line (1980) — north-south divide; now outdated as NEEs have developed
Development gap causes: colonialism, unfair trade, debt cycle, physical factors, conflict
Fairtrade — above-market price + Premium; helps farmers but benefits limited
HIPC debt relief — 36 countries' debts cancelled; Uganda abolished school fees
Grameen Bank microfinance — small loans mainly to women; 97% repayment rate
Extreme poverty: 36% (1990) → 8.6% (2022); but progress mainly in Asia
SDGs — 17 UN goals, agreed 2015, target 2030; cover poverty, health, environment
